issues.title: OC Board of Supervisors District 4 — Four-Way Primary
issues.summary:
⚡ UPDATE (May 7, 2026): FORM 460 FILINGS REVEAL UPDATED SPENDING AND OUTSIDE MONEY. Ballots are in voters' hands — 13 DAYS until primary.

Four candidates are in the race to replace termed-out Supervisor Doug Chaffee (D) in District 4 (Anaheim portion, Brea, Buena Park, Fullerton, La Habra, Placentia, Stanton):

• Fred Jung (Fullerton Mayor) — Re-registered as No Party Preference. ⚡ UPDATED: $347,000 cash on hand (as of April 18). Raised $58,000 since Jan 1, spent $128,000 on advertising. Endorsed by Fullerton firefighters and police unions, Lincoln Club. COMMANDING FINANCIAL LEAD.
• Connor Traut (Buena Park Vice Mayor) — ⚡ UPDATED: $117,000 cash on hand. Spent $136,000 since Jan 1 (HIGHEST SPENDING RATE), raised $29,000. Endorsed by OC Democratic Party, outgoing Supervisor Chaffee, OC Professional Firefighters, carpenters/electricians unions.
• Rose Espinoza (La Habra council) — Self-funded: $150,000 self-loan plus $44,000 in nonmonetary contributions (postcard printing from her nonprofit Rosie's Garage). Endorsed by LA County Fire Fighters Local 1014.
• Tim Shaw (County Board of Education trustee) — ⚡ UPDATED: Spent only $14,000, raised $16,000. BUT: National Association of Realtors is spending $125,000+ in advertising supporting Shaw. Endorsed by OC Republican Party.

District demographics: 341,494 voters — 40.4% Democrat, 30.5% Republican, 23.1% No Party Preference.

⚡ OUTSIDE MONEY ALERT: The $125K National Association of Realtors spend on Shaw's behalf significantly changes the dynamics — Shaw's personal fundraising is modest but he has major institutional backing. Jung's $347K war chest gives him staying power. Traut is spending the fastest.

⚡ MAY 17 UPDATE: LAist published a comprehensive voter guide on the District 4 race. Voice of OC reports campaign spending heating up across all BOS races. Key context: Jung serves as Fullerton mayor AND chairs the Orange County Power Authority and sits on the OCTA board — giving him significant transportation policy experience. Traut, endorsed by the OC Democratic Party and outgoing Supervisor Chaffee, also serves on the OC Fire Authority board. Espinoza has served on the La Habra council since 2000, serving as mayor six times (most recently 2025). Shaw has OC Board of Education experience and OC Republican Party backing plus the $125K Realtor spend.

⚡ MAY 19 UPDATE: 13 DAYS until June 2 primary. Voter registration deadline (May 18) has PASSED — if you are not registered, you can still conditionally register in person at your county elections office or a vote center. Ballots are in voters' hands.

⚡ MAY 21 UPDATE: 12 DAYS until June 2 primary. ⚡ NEW ENDORSEMENT: Orange County Employees Association (OCEA) endorsed Connor Traut — adding to his OC Democratic Party backing. The OC Democratic Party has spent approximately $76,000 advertising Traut's campaign. The race is widely seen as a contest among the three D/left-leaning candidates (Jung, Traut, Espinoza) for who advances alongside Republican Tim Shaw. No public polls of the race have been released. Traut's institutional support (OC Dem Party + OCEA + outgoing Supervisor Chaffee + OC Professional Firefighters) vs. Jung's financial lead ($347K cash) vs. Shaw's Realtor-backed advertising ($125K+) defines the dynamic. Espinoza's self-funded campaign limits her upside.

⚡ MAY 23 UPDATE: 10 DAYS until June 2 primary. Ballots in voters' hands. LAist and Daily Titan published comprehensive voter guides covering all BOS races. Voice of OC reports these are 'OC's most competitive races for the June primary.' Key dynamic unchanged: Jung's financial lead ($347K) vs. Traut's broadest institutional support (OC Dem Party + OCEA + Chaffee + OC Professional Firefighters) vs. Shaw's $125K+ Realtor-backed advertising. Espinoza's self-funded campaign ($150K self-loan) limits her upside. The FY2026-27 Recommended Budget was released May 20 for BOS and public review — the next supervisor inherits a $10.8B annual budget. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH compliance deadline is May 28 (4 DAYS) — fines escalate to $50K/month in June. The enforcement environment is a live issue in this race.

Top-two from the June 2 primary advance to the November 3 general election. The board currently has a 3-2 Democratic majority. If Democrats lose this seat (or District 5), the board flips to Republican majority — affecting the $10.8B annual budget, land use policy, homelessness response, and transportation funding.

⚡ MAY 24 UPDATE: 9 DAYS until June 2 primary. Ballots in voters' hands. The financial dynamics remain: Jung's $347K war chest vs. Traut's broadest institutional support (OC Dem Party + OCEA + Chaffee + OC Professional Firefighters) vs. Shaw's $125K+ Realtor-backed advertising. Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 (4 DAYS) — fines escalate to $50K/month in June. The next supervisor inherits the $10.8B annual budget and the FY2026-27 Recommended Budget released May 20.

⚡ MAY 25 UPDATE: 8 DAYS until June 2 primary. Ballots in voters' hands. ⚡ OC Board of Supervisors voted unanimously on May 6 to deny an appeal against Saddleback Meadows — clearing the way for a 181-home development on 222 acres near Live Oak Canyon/El Toro Road that has been mired in opposition for over four decades. This is a bellwether for how the next D4 supervisor handles development conflicts. Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 (3 DAYS) — court-ordered fines escalate to $50K/month in June if the city remains noncompliant. The next supervisor inherits the $10.8B annual budget.

⚡ MAY 26 UPDATE: 7 DAYS until June 2 primary. Memorial Day — campaigns at lower intensity but mail ballots continue arriving. Financial dynamics unchanged: Jung's $347K war chest vs. Traut's broadest institutional support (OC Dem Party + OCEA + Chaffee + OC Professional Firefighters) vs. Shaw's $125K+ Realtor-backed advertising. ⚡ Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 (2 DAYS) — court-ordered fines escalate to $50K/month in June. Saddleback Meadows approval (May 6) remains the bellwether for how the next D4 supervisor handles development. The next supervisor inherits the $10.8B annual budget and FY2026-27 Recommended Budget.

⚡ MAY 27 UPDATE: 6 DAYS until June 2 primary. Final week of campaigning underway. ⚡ OC PIT COUNT CONTEXT: Homelessness down 13.7% to 6,321 — for the first time, more people sheltered (3,256) than unsheltered (3,065). The next D4 supervisor inherits this progress and must sustain it. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH: Court ordered $160K in penalties; city must adopt compliant housing element by May 28 (TOMORROW) or face $50K/month fines starting June. The housing enforcement environment is a defining issue for this race. Heavy outside spending continues: NAR $125K for Shaw, Lincoln Club for Jung, OC Dem Party $76K for Traut. County budget pressures from Airport Fire payouts, sexual harassment settlements, and federal funding cuts add fiscal complexity for the next supervisor.

public_comment.instructions: Register to vote for June 2 primary. Research all four candidates on housing, transit, and budget positions. Consider donations and volunteering for preferred candidate.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ 6 DAYS to primary. BALLOTS IN VOTERS' HANDS. ⚡ OC homeless count down 13.7% — for the first time more sheltered than unsheltered. The next D4 supervisor inherits this progress. Heavy outside spending: NAR $125K for Shaw, Lincoln Club for Jung, OC Dem Party $76K for Traut. Jung has the financial edge ($347K cash) but Traut has the broadest institutional support. Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 (TOMORROW) — fines escalate to $50K/month. County budget pressures from Airport Fire payouts and federal funding cuts add fiscal complexity. The Board controls OC's $10.8B budget — this race shapes every policy priority.

issues.title: State Senate SD-34 — Open Seat (Tom Umberg termed out)
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 2026): CORRECTION — Josh Newman is NOT running for SD-34. He is running for Superintendent of Public Instruction. The race is now a two-person contest:

• Avelino Valencia (D) — Current Assemblymember for AD-68, frontrunner. Son of immigrant parents, played college football, served in the Assembly since 2022 focusing on consumer protection, gun violence reduction, and government transparency.
• Rhonda Shader (R) — Former Placentia mayor, runner-up for this district in 2022.

The district covers northern OC including most of Santa Ana, Anaheim, Placentia, Fullerton, Buena Park, La Habra, and the west side of Orange. With only one Democrat (Valencia) and one Republican (Shader), both will advance from the June 2 top-two primary to the November general election. Valencia's move to the Senate opens AD-68. The state Senate shapes California's housing mandate framework (SB 9, RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy) that drives the core YIMBY fight in Orange County.

public_comment.instructions: Register to vote for June 2 primary. Research candidates' housing positions.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ 6 DAYS to June 2 primary. Now a straight D-vs-R race. With Newman out (running for Superintendent), Valencia (D) and Shader (R) will both advance from the June primary. The general election in November will be the real contest. Valencia's Assembly record on housing and governance is the key evaluation point. The state Senate shapes RHNA enforcement and Builder's Remedy — making this seat important for the OC housing fight.

issues.title: State Assembly AD-68 — Open Seat (Valencia Running for Senate)
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 2026): Fundraising and endorsement picture is clearer.

• David Peñaloza (D) — Santa Ana city councilor. Endorsed by incumbent Avelino Valencia. Has raised $142,897 — nearly double Lopez's total. Organizational advantage from Valencia's endorsement and network.

• Jessie Lopez (D) — Santa Ana city councilor. Endorsed by the California Legislative Women's Caucus (announced Feb 26, 2026). Has raised $73,737.

With two Democrats and no Republican filed, the June 2 primary will determine the general election matchup. Both candidates come from Santa Ana city council, so differentiation is on policy specifics, community connections, and institutional support.

public_comment.instructions: Register to vote for June 2 primary. Research both candidates' housing and transit positions.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ 6 DAYS to June 2 primary. The Assemblymember for AD-68 votes on state housing legislation that shapes the OC YIMBY fight. ⚡ Heavy outside spending: Penaloza has $1M+ from tech interests; Lopez has $118K from SEIU plus $400K in negative ads against Penaloza. Evaluate their city council records on housing development and state mandate compliance.

issues.title: California Governor's Race — Housing is #1 Issue, Dem Fragmentation Risk
issues.summary:
⚡ MAJOR UPDATE (May 7, 2026): Three debates down — CNN debate was the most-watched and most combative. BALLOTS ARE IN VOTERS' HANDS. 13 DAYS until primary.

⚡ DEBATE #3 RECAP (MAY 5, CNN, East Los Angeles College): Seven candidates debated — Becerra (D), Bianco (R), Hilton (R), Mahan (D), Porter (D), Steyer (D), Villaraigosa (D). Moderated by Kaitlan Collins and Elex Michaelson. Two hours, highly combative.

KEY TAKEAWAYS:
• BECERRA TOOK THE MOST HITS — after his polling surge, every candidate targeted him. Critics hammered his changed position on single-payer healthcare and the corruption scandal involving his former chief of staff Sean McCluskie.
• CHAD BIANCO DOMINATED CONSERVATIVE MESSAGING — the Republican sheriff received significant airtime pushing hard-right talking points on immigration and sanctuary policies.
• HEALTHCARE, TRUMP, AND COST-OF-LIVING DOMINATED — Republicans blamed decades of Democratic control; Democrats deflected by criticizing Trump. Becerra argued gas prices rose 'one to two dollars because of Donald Trump and his war in Iran.'
• PORTER ADDRESSED HER TEMPERAMENT CONTROVERSY; Mahan quipped about high-speed rail costs exceeding moon missions.
• NO CLEAR WINNER — the debate deepened fragmentation rather than consolidating any lane.

DEBATE #1 RECAP (APRIL 22): Six candidates, San Francisco, Nexstar/KTLA5. No clear winner.
DEBATE #2 RECAP (APRIL 28): Eight candidates, Pomona College/CBS. Expanded field deepened D fragmentation.

⚡ POLLING — BECERRA LEADS IVN, HILTON LEADS EMERSON:
IVN POLL (April 20): Becerra 23%, Hilton 19.7%, Bianco 16.7%, Steyer 13.9%, Porter 11.4%
EMERSON POLL (April 16): Hilton 17%, Bianco 14%, Steyer 14%, Becerra 10%, Porter 10%, Mahan 5%, 23% undecided

BECERRA SURGE: Former CA AG and HHS Secretary — campaign reports 1,659% engagement spike, nearly $400K raised since Swalwell's exit. But the CNN debate saw him targeted from all sides, which could stall momentum.

SWALWELL CONTEXT: In April 2026, CNN published allegations from multiple women accusing Swalwell of sexual misconduct; he suspended his campaign but his name STAYS on the June 2 ballot — a Democratic drain that could siphon votes from viable D candidates.

DEMOCRATIC FRAGMENTATION: Five-way D split (Steyer/Becerra/Porter/Mahan/Villaraigosa) persists. Thurmond failed to qualify for CNN. The risk remains that two Republicans make the top-two, shutting Democrats out of the November general.

VOTER ISSUES: Economy 41%, housing affordability 20%, threats to democracy 10%, crime 6%.

⚡ BALLOT LOGISTICS: 1.9 million OC ballots loaded onto USPS trailers starting May 2. Mail-in voting open May 5-June 2. Voter registration deadline (May 18) has PASSED — conditional registration still available in person at vote centers. In-person voting June 2 (some early locations open May 30).

Newsom has NOT endorsed. The next governor determines whether California's housing enforcement posture (RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals) continues or weakens — directly affecting the OC housing fight.

⚡ MAY 17 UPDATE: Time Magazine published a 'where the race stands' analysis (May 14) noting that Becerra, Steyer, and Hilton are the three front-runners, with the field still deeply fragmented. With 61 candidates on the ballot and five viable Democrats splitting the vote, the risk of two Republicans (Bianco + Hilton) making the top-two remains real.

⚡ MAY 19 UPDATE: VOTER REGISTRATION DEADLINE (MAY 18) HAS PASSED. NEW POLL from The Hill (published ~May 15-19) shows a VIRTUAL THREE-WAY TIE: Becerra 19%, Hilton 17%, Steyer 17% — all within the margin of error. Chad Bianco and Katie Porter both poll at or above 10%. The five-way Democratic fragmentation persists and is now empirically confirmed as a dead heat at the top. The risk of two Republicans making the top-two remains very real with the D vote split this evenly. 14 DAYS until primary. If you missed the voter registration deadline, you can still conditionally register in person at a vote center.

⚡ MAY 20 UPDATE: NEW EMERSON POLL (May 9-10) confirms Becerra's surge — he topped the field for the first time in an Emerson poll at 19%, up NINE POINTS since mid-April. Hilton and Steyer are tied at 17%. Porter at 10%, Mahan at 8%, 12% undecided. The 2-point Becerra lead is within the margin of error, making this effectively a three-way dead heat. Becerra's momentum is real but fragile — the CNN debate saw him targeted from all sides. 13 DAYS until primary. BALLOTS ARE IN VOTERS' HANDS.

⚡ MAY 21 UPDATE: 12 DAYS until June 2 primary. ⚡ NEW POLL: California Democratic Party final tracking poll (EVITARUS, May 14-16, n=1,200, MOE ±2.83%) shows Hilton at 22%, Becerra at 21%, Steyer at 15%. This is the first poll to show Hilton ahead of Becerra since his surge — and the gap is within the margin of error. ⚡ CORRUPTION BOMBSHELL: At the May 14 CBS Bay Area debate, Becerra's former campaign strategist Dana Williamson PLEADED GUILTY that same day to conspiring to steal from Becerra's campaign. Federal prosecutors consider Becerra a victim, but opponents weaponized it immediately — Katie Porter asked whether Becerra could still face consequences. Steyer's attack ads: 'Was Becerra complicit, or too incompetent to notice?' Becerra responded: 'This is what happens when you take the lead in the polls.' Voter commitment data is key: Hilton voters are most locked in (73% definite) while Becerra (52%) and Steyer (48%) voters are softer — meaning the final 12 days could see significant movement. The most likely general election matchup: Hilton vs. Becerra.

⚡ MAY 24 UPDATE: 9 DAYS until June 2 primary. The race remains a three-way dead heat: most recent polls show Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer all within margin of error. The EVITARUS poll (May 14-16) shows Hilton at 22%, Becerra at 21%, Steyer at 15% — but Becerra's voter commitment (52% definite) is notably softer than Hilton's (73%). The Williamson guilty plea (May 14) continues to be weaponized against Becerra. With just 12% undecided, the final 9 days could see significant movement. The risk of two Republicans (Hilton + Bianco) making the top-two remains real with the D vote split this evenly. BALLOTS ARE IN VOTERS' HANDS.

⚡ MAY 25 UPDATE: 8 DAYS until June 2 primary. Memorial Day weekend — campaign intensity may dip briefly but mail ballots continue arriving. The three-way dead heat persists: Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%, Steyer 15% (EVITARUS, May 14-16). Becerra's voter commitment remains softer (52% definite) than Hilton's (73%), meaning the final week could see significant movement. The five-way Democratic fragmentation (Becerra/Steyer/Porter/Mahan/Villaraigosa) continues to create the risk of two Republicans (Hilton + Bianco) making the top-two. The next governor determines California's housing enforcement posture — RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals — making this upstream of every OC housing fight.

⚡ MAY 26 UPDATE: 7 DAYS until June 2 primary. Memorial Day weekend — campaign intensity dips but mail ballots continue arriving. The three-way dead heat persists: Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%, Steyer 15% (EVITARUS, May 14-16). Becerra's voter commitment (52% definite) remains softer than Hilton's (73%). The Williamson guilty plea continues to be weaponized. Five-way Democratic fragmentation (Becerra/Steyer/Porter/Mahan/Villaraigosa) maintains the risk of two Republicans making top-two. Final week of campaigning begins tomorrow.

⚡ MAY 27 UPDATE: 6 DAYS until June 2 primary. Final week of campaigning underway. Three-way dead heat persists: Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%, Steyer 15%. OC's shifting 'purple county' status makes the governor's race especially consequential locally — the next governor controls RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy, and AG referrals. Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 (TOMORROW) — $50K/month fines start June if noncompliant. The enforcement framework depends on who sits in the governor's office. D fragmentation risk remains acute.

public_comment.instructions: Register to vote for June 2 primary. Research candidates' housing enforcement positions. The top-two primary means strategic voting matters — avoid contributing to Dem fragmentation if housing enforcement is your priority.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ 6 DAYS to primary. TWO POLLS NOW SHOW A DEAD HEAT: EVITARUS (May 14-16) has Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%; Emerson (May 9-10) has Becerra 19%, Hilton/Steyer 17%. Becerra's momentum may be stalling — the Williamson guilty plea gives opponents ammunition, and his voter commitment (52% definite) is softer than Hilton's (73%). The five-way D fragmentation persists. The risk of two Republicans (Hilton + Bianco) making top-two remains real. Evaluate candidates on housing enforcement posture — the governor controls RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy, and AG referrals. This is upstream of every OC housing fight. The Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 (TOMORROW) and $50K/month escalating fines show state enforcement working — the next governor determines whether it continues.

issues.title: City of Orange Housing Element — Newly Compliant, Builder's Remedy Legacy
issues.summary:
UPDATE (March 2026): The City of Orange appears to have achieved Housing Element compliance — it is NOT on Governor Newsom's March 25 list of 15 noncompliant cities (92% of CA communities are now compliant statewide). The city re-adopted its Housing Element in October 2023 after the initial 2023 HCD rejection.

However, the Builder's Remedy legacy remains significant. Before the city regained compliance, developers filed four Builder's Remedy applications totaling 696 housing units: Integral Communities (209 townhomes behind Village of Orange mall), Stonefield Development (138 apartments at Chapman-Yorba site), and two Milan Capital Management projects (118 townhomes at Mara Brandman Horse Arena, 231 units at Sully-Miller quarry). The city pushed back on these applications, requiring zoning changes and general plan amendments — tactics similar to those struck down by LA County judges in other Builder's Remedy cases.

Additionally, Californians for Homeownership (realtor-backed) sued the city alleging the approved plan included parcels with deed restrictions that forbid housing. AB 1893 (effective 2025) formalized Builder's Remedy as an entitlement pathway and prevents cities from requiring extra approvals for these projects — strengthening the legal position of the pending applications.

public_comment.instructions: Attend City of Orange council meetings. Monitor Builder's Remedy application proceedings. Check cityoforange.org for agenda.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ The fight has shifted: Orange is technically compliant now, but the real battle is over the 696 Builder's Remedy units filed during noncompliance. AB 1893 strengthens developers' legal position — the city can't require extra approvals. The Californians for Homeownership lawsuit adds another pressure vector. Monitor whether the city processes these applications in good faith or continues obstruction. The November 2026 council elections (3 seats + mayor) will determine whether Orange's housing posture improves or regresses. This is no longer about forcing compliance — it's about ensuring the compliance produces actual housing.

issues.title: Irvine General Plan Adopted — 57,656 Units, Now Implementation Phase
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 14, 2026): The Irvine Planning Commission UNANIMOUSLY voted on March 19 to recommend a zoning change enabling the Irvine Company's revised Oak Creek development plan. The zoning change creates an 'Alternative Open Space Approach' for Planning Area 12, which commissioners said provides 'more widely usable public benefit' than the restricted-use golf course designation. City Council is expected to hear this item imminently — Mayor Larry Agran has signaled he will ensure residents can vote on the project via a future ballot measure, creating uncertainty about whether the council will approve outright or punt to voters in November 2026.

The Irvine Company's revised offer includes: $96 million in community benefit fees (up from $90.4M), 2,000 rent vouchers ($72 million value), land donations, and a 50-acre nature park creating a trail corridor from Limestone Canyon through central Irvine to Quail Hill. The proposal includes a new elementary school within the 3,100-unit residential village.

The Irvine General Plan (ADOPTED August 13, 2024) with the full 57,656-unit scenario remains in implementation phase. Key areas: 26,607 units in Spectrum, 15,000 at Irvine Business Complex, 5,252 at Great Park Neighborhoods Transit Village.

Opposition: Former mayors Sally Anne Sheridan and Christina Shea are gathering signatures for a citizen's initiative (~19,000 needed), arguing the golf course is protected under a 1988 voter-approved open space initiative. The Woodbridge Village Association board continues to oppose the conversion.

⚡ NEW (May 2026): In addition to the Oak Creek conversion, the Irvine Planning Commission approved a zoning change allowing up to 2,500 new housing units near UC Irvine — the Irvine Company proposed converting the privately operated site from office use to residential, leveraging its proximity to major employment centers and the university. Combined with the 3,100-unit Oak Creek plan, Irvine is advancing 5,600+ new housing units across two major developments.

The November 2026 council elections (3 seats + mayor) remain critical — if the 5-2 Democratic majority shifts, implementation of the General Plan could be slowed.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor Irvine council for General Plan implementation decisions. Track Oak Creek Golf Course conversion hearings. Support pro-housing council candidates for November 2026.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ The Planning Commission's unanimous recommendation is a strong signal. The Irvine Company's revised package — $96M in fees, 2,000 rent vouchers, nature park — makes this harder to oppose. But the signature-gathering effort for a ballot initiative could delay everything. The City Council vote will be the next decisive moment. Even from outside Irvine, Irvine's housing supply directly affects OC-wide rents and availability.

issues.title: OC Streetcar — In Street Testing, Spring 2026 Launch Target
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 22, 2026): The OC Streetcar ($649M project, 4.15 miles, Santa Ana to Garden Grove) is 92-95% complete and in active street testing. Track is finished along the entire route. Street testing began February 20 on Santa Ana Boulevard between Raitt Street and Sasscer Park, with daytime testing underway as of April 8 — the streetcar is now running on city streets during regular hours.

LAUNCH TARGET REVISED TO AUGUST 2026. Despite earlier 'spring 2026' language, OCTA's operational timeline points to an August launch date. Eight Siemens S700 vehicles (capacity: 211 passengers each, 62 seated) have been delivered. Travel time: 22 minutes with 10-15 minute frequency across 10 stops in each direction. Estimated daily ridership: 7,300. Fares: $2 one way or $5 day pass. Service planned daily from 6am to 11pm with extended weekend hours. The project has faced years of delays (originally expected 2021) including human remains discovery and contractor lawsuits. Funded by federal, state, and local dollars including Measure M.

OC's first modern rail transit line is in its final testing phase.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor launch and ridership data. OCTA board meetings are public.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ The OC Streetcar is 95% complete with an August 1 target launch — just 72 days away. OCTA CEO Johnson: 'This is the beginning of something, whether we go north on Harbor Boulevard or south on Bristol Street.' For a transit advocate, success builds the case for expanded OC rail transit. Failure becomes ammunition against future investment. Monitor the launch and early ridership data. This is a watch item, not an action item.

issues.title: OC Homelessness — Grand Jury Criticism of County Approach
issues.summary:
The 2025 Orange County Grand Jury found that the county's approach to homelessness 'remains mostly reactive' rather than preventive. The report criticized fragmented services, insufficient data tracking, and lack of coordination between the county and its 34 cities. Orange County's homeless population is concentrated in Santa Ana and the Santa Ana River corridor. The Board of Supervisors majority (currently 3-2 D) shapes the county's response, making the District 4 and 5 elections directly relevant to this issue.

**May 27 update:** The 2026 PIT Count results show significant progress: 6,321 total homeless (down 13.7% from 7,322 in 2024). HISTORIC: For the first time, more people sheltered (3,256) than unsheltered (3,065). Unsheltered count down 27%. 8% reduction since 2019. However, advocates note anti-camping enforcement may influence counts and less than 1 in 5 shelter residents transition to permanent housing. The Grand Jury's prevention-over-reaction framework remains the benchmark. 6 DAYS to June 2 primary — the D4/D5 races determine whether this progress is sustained.

public_comment.instructions: Board of Supervisors meetings. Written comments accepted via email.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ PIT COUNT (May 2026): OC homelessness down 13.7% to 6,321 — for the first time more sheltered (3,256) than unsheltered (3,065). Unsheltered down 27%. 8% reduction since 2019. Progress is real but fragile — less than 1 in 5 shelter residents exit to permanent housing. The Grand Jury critique mirrors the Austin Safe & Sound approach — accountability through data and outcomes tracking. Housing supply is upstream of homelessness. The board majority determines whether the county takes a reactive or proactive approach. 6 DAYS to the D4/D5 primary that determines board composition.

issues.title: OC Board of Supervisors District 5 — Foley (D) vs Dixon (R)
issues.summary:
⚡ UPDATE (May 7, 2026): FORM 460 FILINGS + OUTSIDE SPENDING REVEAL THE MOST EXPENSIVE SUPERVISOR RACE IN OC. Ballots are in voters' hands — 13 DAYS until primary.

Incumbent Supervisor Katrina Foley (D), currently Board Vice Chair, faces a challenge from Assemblywoman Diane Dixon (R) in District 5.

⚡ UPDATED CAMPAIGN FINANCE (as of April 18):
• FOLEY: Spent $222,000 from her campaign since Jan 1. Cash on hand: $225,000+. PLUS: Association of Orange County Deputy Sheriffs spending $213,000 in union advertising supporting Foley.
• DIXON: Spent $117,000. Cash on hand: $232,000+. PLUS: Lincoln Club spending $36,000+ supporting Dixon, $18,000 supporting educator Lucy Vellema, and $11,600 opposing Foley — totaling $66,000 in Lincoln Club spending in this race.

⚡ MOST MONEY IN SOUTH OC: Voice of OC reports that 'most of that money is headed toward South Orange County' — this race is attracting more outside spending than any other supervisor contest.

District demographics: 436,228 voters — 38.1% R, 32.8% D, 22.1% NPP. Most heavily Republican of the five supervisor districts.

Foley's background: former Costa Mesa mayor (first directly elected, 2018), attorney. Won her first full term in 2022 by a 51-49 margin.

Dixon's background: current Assemblywoman (elected 2022, reelected 2024), former Newport Beach city council member (two terms as mayor). Endorsed by State Senators Tony Strickland and Steven Choi.

How close is this district? Kamala Harris won District 5 by just 318 votes (49-48%) in 2024. If Dixon wins, the board reverts to Republican majority.

District 5 covers: Aliso Viejo, Costa Mesa, Dana Point, a large portion of Irvine, Laguna Beach, Laguna Hills, Laguna Niguel, Laguna Woods, Newport Beach, San Clemente, San Juan Capistrano.

⚡ MAY 24 UPDATE: 9 DAYS to June 2 primary. Ballots in voters' hands. This race remains the most expensive supervisor contest in OC, with $213K in deputy sheriff union spending for Foley and $66K+ in Lincoln Club spending for Dixon/Vellema/against Foley. The FY2026-27 Recommended Budget is now available for public review. Dixon has endorsements from 3 sitting supervisors (Chaffee, Wagner, Nguyen). Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 (4 DAYS) — the enforcement environment is a live campaign issue.

⚡ MAY 25 UPDATE: 8 DAYS to June 2 primary. Ballots in voters' hands. LAist published a comprehensive voter guide identifying this as one of OC's most competitive races — Foley is running for reelection against Assemblywoman Dixon and educator Lucy Vellema. District 5 is the most heavily Republican of the five supervisor districts (38.1% R, 32.8% D) — Kamala Harris won it by just 318 votes in 2024. Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 (3 DAYS) — the housing enforcement environment remains a live campaign issue. If Dixon wins, the board flips to Republican majority.

⚡ MAY 26 UPDATE: 7 DAYS to June 2 primary. Memorial Day — mail ballots continue arriving. Financial dynamics stable: Foley ($225K+ cash + $213K deputy sheriff union support) vs. Dixon ($232K+ cash + $66K+ Lincoln Club backing). ⚡ Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 (2 DAYS) — housing enforcement posture is a live campaign issue. If Dixon wins, the board flips to Republican majority. D5 remains the most heavily Republican supervisor district (38.1% R).

⚡ MAY 27 UPDATE: 6 DAYS until June 2 primary. Final week of campaigning underway. ⚡ OC PIT COUNT CONTEXT: Homelessness down 13.7% to 6,321 — for the first time, more people sheltered (3,256) than unsheltered (3,065). Foley can cite this progress against Dixon's public safety framing. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH: Court ordered $160K in penalties; compliance deadline is May 28 (TOMORROW) — $50K/month fines start June. Housing enforcement is a defining campaign issue. ⚡ HEAVY OUTSIDE SPENDING: Unions have spent $565K supporting Foley; Lincoln Club $163K against Foley/for Dixon. County budget pressures from Airport Fire payouts, sexual harassment settlements, and federal funding cuts add fiscal complexity. If Dixon wins, the board flips to Republican majority regardless of D4 outcome.

public_comment.instructions: Register to vote for June 2 primary. Research both candidates. This is a top-two primary — both will likely advance to November given it's a two-person race.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ 6 DAYS to primary. BALLOTS IN VOTERS' HANDS. This race is attracting the most outside money in OC — unions have spent $565K supporting Foley; Lincoln Club $163K against Foley/for Dixon. Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 (TOMORROW) — $50K/month fines start June. OC homeless count down 13.7% — Foley can cite progress; Dixon frames public safety differently. County budget pressures from Airport Fire payouts and federal funding cuts add complexity. If Foley loses, the board flips regardless of District 4.

issues.title: Governor Newsom's Final Warning to 15 Noncompliant Cities
issues.summary:
On March 25, 2026, Governor Newsom issued final warnings to 15 cities and counties that remain noncompliant with state Housing Element law — more than two years behind schedule with no path to compliance within 60 days. The 15 jurisdictions (Atwater, Avenal, California City, Corcoran, Escalon, Half Moon Bay, Hanford, Kings County, Lemoore, Merced County, Montclair, Oakdale, Patterson, Ridgecrest, Turlock) have 30 days to respond before referral to the Attorney General.

Key context: 92% of California communities (480 cities/counties) have achieved compliance. The Housing Accountability Unit has taken 1,200+ enforcement actions and 'unlocked' 12,486 housing units including 3,644 affordable units. Critically, the City of Orange is NOT on the noncompliant list — confirming it has achieved compliance since its 2023 rejection.

Newsom: 'No community gets a pass when it comes to addressing homelessness or creating more housing access.'

⚡ MAY 21 UPDATE — HUNTINGTON BEACH FINED $160,000: On May 15, a court ordered Huntington Beach to pay $160,000 in penalties for over 4.5 years of noncompliance with state housing element law (deadline was October 2021). Monthly fines of $10,000 have been running since January 2025. ⚡ FINES ESCALATE TO $50,000/MONTH starting June 2026 if the city remains noncompliant — compliance deadline: MAY 28 (4 DAYS). The U.S. Supreme Court declined in February 2026 to hear HB's challenge. Governor Newsom: 'Citizens in this community should be appalled by their city leaders' actions here.' AG Bonta: 'This civil penalty is a costly lesson that no city is above the law.' This is the most aggressive enforcement action yet against an OC city — and it's in one of the most NIMBY-resistant jurisdictions in Southern California. Newport Beach residents are also pushing a ballot measure (Responsible Housing Initiative) to reduce their RHNA-mandated 8,000+ units to under 3,000. Meanwhile, Yorba Linda proactively passed zoning changes (Measure JJ, 90%+ voter support) to meet requirements. The contrast between compliance and resistance is stark.

public_comment.instructions: This is a state-level enforcement action. Monitor for Attorney General referrals and their impact on the statewide housing compliance framework.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ HUNTINGTON BEACH compliance deadline is May 28 — TOMORROW. Fines escalate from $10K/month to $50K/month starting June 2026. Total accumulated fines: $160K. Over 4.5 years behind schedule. US Supreme Court declined HB's challenge (Feb 2026). AG Bonta: 'No city is above the law.' Huntington Beach's $160K fine and escalation to $50K/month is the most tangible proof yet that state enforcement works. The contrast is instructive: Yorba Linda complied proactively (90%+ voter support); Huntington Beach fought and is now paying $50K/month. Orange achieved compliance but is still obstructing Builder's Remedy units. The enforcement framework is the backbone of every OC housing fight — and the governor's race determines whether it continues. The June 2 primary is 6 DAYS AWAY.

issues.title: NEW: CD-40 — Young Kim vs Ken Calvert (R-vs-R Incumbent Clash)
issues.summary:
NEW (April 2026): Proposition 50 (approved Nov 2025) redrew California's congressional districts, creating a new CD-40 that pits two Republican incumbents against each other: Rep. Young Kim (current CD-40) and Rep. Ken Calvert (current CD-41). Calvert represents 51% of the new district's population; Kim represents 35%.

Fundraising: Kim has a commanding $2 million+ cash advantage over Calvert, outraising him in Q4 2025. The new district spans eastern Orange County cities (Villa Park, Mission Viejo) through the Inland Empire (Murrieta, Menifee).

Ten candidates total are in the June 2 top-two primary, including Democrats Joe Kerr and Esther Kim Varet. The primary dynamics matter: if both Republicans advance, a D could be shut out of the November general. If a Democrat makes top-two alongside one Republican, the general election becomes competitive in a district that includes OC territory.

public_comment.instructions: Register to vote for June 2 primary. Research candidates' housing and infrastructure positions.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ 6 DAYS to June 2 primary. Voter registration deadline (May 18) has PASSED. Prop 50's redistricting reshuffled OC's federal representation. The Kim-Calvert clash is unusual — two incumbents in the same party fighting for the same seat. For housing advocates, the key question is which candidates (of all 10) best support federal housing and transit funding. Monitor whether a Democrat can make the top-two — that would make this competitive in November.

issues.title: OC Homelessness Enforcement — Anti-Camping Ordinance & 2026 PIT Count
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): Following the Supreme Court's Grants Pass ruling, Orange County has significantly escalated homelessness enforcement — but new state funding and housing initiatives create an opportunity.

⚡ NEW STATE FUNDING (April 8): Governor Newsom announced $145.4M in HHAP funding statewide. OC (including Anaheim, Irvine, Santa Ana, and its CoC) will receive $35.1M for homelessness prevention and housing programs — rapid rehousing, rental assistance, a 325-bed low-barrier shelter, and supportive services including case management and prevention programs. The Board of Supervisors is requesting Round 7 statewide HHAP funding of $500M for 2026-27, plus $500M current fiscal year and $1B ongoing annually.

⚡ NEW (April 6): OC Housing Authority opened Project-Based Voucher (PBV) applications for people experiencing homelessness at 1400 Bristol St, Costa Mesa — 40 one-bedroom and 8 studio units, with 24-unit admission preference for CES referrals.

⚡ RHNA COMPLIANCE ALARM (April 2026): Voice of OC reports that many OC cities are far behind on state affordable housing targets. Anaheim has permitted only 3,048 of its required 17,453 units — and is over 3,000 low-income units short. Fullerton has credit for just 1,384 of its 13,209-unit RHNA obligation, with 11,825 units still to go. Multiple large OC cities including Huntington Beach, Costa Mesa, Newport Beach, Garden Grove, Tustin, Westminster, and Fountain Valley are also behind schedule. The compliance gap threatens Builder's Remedy exposure and undercuts the housing supply needed to address homelessness upstream.

⚡ BUDGET PRESSURES (April 14): Board of Supervisors approved nine state budget funding priorities, including support for the proposed $10B state Affordable Housing Bond. Chair Chaffee warned of a 'perfect storm' of fiscal pressures from reduced federal healthcare funding and unfunded state mandates.

• November 2025: Board of Supervisors passed anti-camping ordinance (4-1 vote). Multiple cities adopted similar local ordinances.
• March 2026: Davis Vanguard reported OC residents serving jail time for unlawful camping violations.
• January 26-29, 2026: 2026 Point-in-Time (PIT) Count conducted. ⚡ RESULTS PUBLISHED MAY 19 (see below).
• 2026 NOFA: $20.9 million in supportive housing funding released, plus up to 100 project-based vouchers.
• Grand Jury recommendation: OC should prioritize homelessness prevention over reactive measures by June 30, 2026.
• From July 2021 to February 2026: 449 encampments have been removed countywide.

⚡ MAY 19 UPDATE: The OC Board of Supervisors UNANIMOUSLY backed AB 1907, which would exempt affordable housing, supportive housing, and emergency shelters from CEQA (California Environmental Quality Act). If signed into law, AB 1907 would remove a major barrier that delays and increases costs for homeless-serving housing projects. The unanimous vote signals bipartisan support for streamlining housing production for the most vulnerable populations.

⚡ MAY 19 — PIT COUNT RESULTS PUBLISHED: Total counted: 6,321 (DOWN 13.7% from 7,322 in 2024). HISTORIC MILESTONE: For the first time, more people were counted in shelters/transitional housing (3,256, up 3%) than on the streets (3,065, down 26.6%). 10-year trend: 8% reduction since 2019. Key demographics: 882 seniors (62+), 245 youth (18-24), 206 veterans. Substance abuse: 45% unsheltered / 26% sheltered. Serious mental illness: 33% unsheltered / 38% sheltered. About 25% live in vehicles. Unemployment: 63.56%. OC Office of Care Coordination Director Doug Becht: 'Over the last 10 years we've built an effective and comprehensive system of care.' Advocate David Duran (Housing is a Human Right OC) called it 'a guestimate,' noting anti-camping enforcement may influence numbers and less than 1 in 5 shelter residents transition to permanent housing.

The $35.1M state injection and PBV applications are exactly the kind of service-side investment the Grand Jury called for. But the 'perfect storm' of federal funding cuts creates fiscal headwinds — and the RHNA compliance shortfalls show the housing supply side is failing. The Board of Supervisors majority (3-2 D) shapes whether this funding is deployed effectively. The District 4 and 5 races directly affect this balance.

public_comment.instructions: Board of Supervisors meetings. Monitor Grand Jury response deadline (June 30). Track PIT Count results when published.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ PIT COUNT RESULTS ARE IN: 6,321 total (down 13.7%), with more people in shelters (3,256) than on streets (3,065) for the first time. Unsheltered count down 27%. 8% reduction since 2019. This is progress — but context matters. Anti-camping enforcement may be pushing people out of count areas, and less than 1 in 5 shelter residents exit to permanent housing. The Grand Jury's call for prevention over reaction remains the right framework. The dual-track approach mirrors Austin Safe & Sound: shelter expansion (sheltered count up 3%) paired with housing pathways (which remain inadequate). The Board majority determines whether enforcement is paired with services. This connects directly to the District 4/5 races — 6 DAYS to primary.

issues.title: NEW: Saddleback Meadows — Board of Supervisors Clears 181-Home Development After 40+ Years
issues.summary:
⚡ NEW (May 6, 2026): The Orange County Board of Supervisors voted UNANIMOUSLY to deny an appeal challenging Saddleback Meadows, a 181-home development planned on 222 acres near the intersection of Live Oak Canyon Road and El Toro Road. The decision effectively clears the way for a project that has been mired in revisions, lawsuits, and community opposition for MORE THAN FOUR DECADES.

The appeal was filed in February by lawyers for the Saddleback Canyons Conservancy and Rural Canyons Conservation Fund, who argued the project would negatively impact the area. The current 181-home version was originally approved in 2002 — the supervisors' unanimous vote to deny the appeal means the project can finally proceed.

Trabuco Canyon residents had previously accused the Board of treating the appeal hearing as a 'done deal,' according to Voice of OC. Despite community opposition, the project ultimately survived decades of legal and political challenges.

This is a significant housing supply signal: even in South OC's most contested terrain, the Board of Supervisors is clearing development — consistent with the current 3-2 D majority's posture on housing.

public_comment.instructions: Appeal denied. The project can proceed to construction. Monitor for any further legal challenges.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ The unanimous vote is notable — even the Republican supervisors joined in clearing this 40+ year delayed project. It signals that the current Board majority supports housing development even in contentious rural-suburban areas. The 181 units won't solve OC's housing crisis alone, but the precedent matters: NIMBYism can delay but can't permanently block housing when elected officials have the will to act. This is relevant context as the District 4 and 5 races approach — the board composition determines whether this posture continues.

issues.title: NEW: OC Releases $20.9M Supportive Housing NOFA + Affordable Housing Month
issues.summary:
NEW (May 2026): Orange County Housing and Community Development released the 2026 Supportive Housing Notice of Funding Availability (NOFA) — $20.9 MILLION in combined federal and state funding for supportive housing:

• Federal HOME Investment Partnership Program (HOME)
• HOME American Rescue Plan Program (HOME-ARP)
• Mental Health Services Act (MHSA) / Behavioral Health Services Act (BHSA)
• 15G Reserves and Housing Successor Agency (HSA) Funds
• Up to 100 Housing Choice, Mainstream and/or Veterans Affairs Supportive Housing Project Based Vouchers

Funding supports acquisition, new construction, and acquisition/rehabilitation of Supportive Housing for extremely low-income households who are homeless.

Additionally, OC is holding Affordable and Fair Housing Months in April-May 2026, featuring educational resources, workshops, affordable housing groundbreakings and grand openings. The 2026 Affordable Housing Awards celebration is scheduled for May 14.

⚡ NEW (May 2026): The Orion, a 166-unit affordable housing community, opened in the City of Orange — developed by USA Properties Fund and Riverside Charitable Corp. The project includes 8 permanent supportive housing units for residents facing housing insecurity and was approved for an affordable housing density bonus, significantly increasing allowed apartments per acre. Also in May, Mission Viejo advanced a housing development near Saddleback College — the city selected Irvine-based Trumark Homes for a ~215-unit project on 6 developable acres, with a 120-day Property Access Agreement for environmental and technical studies before a formal purchase agreement returns to council this fall.

Combined with the $35.1M HHAP state funding and the $10B proposed state Affordable Housing Bond, this represents the largest injection of housing funding in recent OC history.

public_comment.instructions: Housing developers and nonprofits can apply for NOFA funding through OCHCD. Monitor ochcd.org/housing-development for application deadlines and details.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ $20.9M in supportive housing funding is significant — especially the up to 100 Project Based Vouchers for extremely low-income homeless households. This is the supply-side investment that complements the enforcement approach. For OC Housing Now's RHNA compliance tracking campaign, monitor which cities and projects receive these funds. The May 14 Affordable Housing Awards may highlight best practices worth amplifying.

issues.title: OC Releases $20.9M Supportive Housing NOFA — Federal HOME Funds Available
issues.summary: NEW (May 2026): OC Housing and Community Development (OCHCD) announced the release of the 2026 Supportive Housing Notice of Funding Availability (NOFA), making up to $20.9 million in Federal HOME Investment Partnership Program funds and other programs available to promote the acquisition, new construction, and acquisition/rehabilitation of Supportive Housing. This is the largest single supportive housing funding release in recent OC history. Additionally, Orange County (including Anaheim, Irvine, Santa Ana and its Continuum of Care) will receive $35.1 million in Homeless Housing, Assistance and Prevention (HHAP) program funding from the state. Combined, these two funding streams provide over $56 million for housing the county's most vulnerable residents. The Orange County Housing Authority also began accepting applications for Project Based Voucher (PBV) units for people experiencing homelessness on April 6, 2026.

public_comment.instructions: NOFA applications open through OCHCD. Developers and nonprofits can apply at ochcd.org.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The $20.9M NOFA plus $35.1M in HHAP funding represents serious money flowing into OC's supportive housing pipeline. This is the implementation side of the policy advocacy — tracking which projects receive funding and whether they get built is how you measure progress. The PBV voucher program opening for homeless applicants is a direct service delivery win.

issues.title: OC Affordability Reality Check — 25,000 Units Built, Only 17% Affordable
issues.summary: NEW (May 2026): A Voice of OC analysis reveals that over 25,000 housing units were built in Orange County between 2021-2024, but only 17% (4,361 homes) qualify as affordable. Santa Ana and Anaheim account for 47% of OC's low-income housing built in that period. Some cities like Aliso Viejo and Huntington Beach haven't produced any affordable units. The median price for an existing single-family home in Orange County is $1,467,500 (March 2026 CAR data). Under the state's latest RHNA housing planning cycle, OC jurisdictions must collectively zone for over 180,000 new homes — with 75,000 designated for very low and low income families. The gap between zoning capacity and actual affordable production remains the central challenge for housing advocates.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor individual city housing element compliance and RHNA progress reports.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This data exposes the gap between OC's housing production and its affordability needs. Only 17% affordable out of 25,000 units means the market is building — but not for the people who need it most. The RHNA mandate for 75,000 low-income units creates a compliance framework, but actual production depends on funding (like the $20.9M NOFA) and political will at the city level. Huntington Beach and Aliso Viejo's zero affordable units should be a focal point for accountability campaigns.

issues.title: California Governor's Race — Housing is #1 Issue, YIMBY Endorsements Shape Field
issues.summary: UPDATE (May 2026): Housing has emerged as the dominant issue in California's 2026 governor's race, with most candidates aligning around pro-housing positions. YIMBY Action and Abundant Housing LA have endorsed Tom Steyer, citing his commitment to addressing the housing crisis. The Democratic field fragmentation remains a risk — multiple strong candidates splitting the progressive vote could allow a Republican or moderate to advance through the jungle primary. Governor Newsom's recent $145.4M investment in homelessness reduction funding (including $35.1M for Orange County) signals the outgoing administration's priorities. The next governor will oversee RHNA enforcement, housing element compliance, and the future of California's YIMBY legislative agenda (SB 840, density bonus law, Builder's Remedy).

public_comment.instructions: Primary election. Research candidates' housing platforms. Vote.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ✦ 6 DAYS to primary. OC's shift to 'purple county' status makes this race especially consequential locally. EVITARUS POLL (May 14-16): Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%. The Williamson guilty plea and HUNTINGTON BEACH's $160K fine ($50K/month escalation starting June, compliance deadline TOMORROW) make the governor's race critical for OC housing. The next governor controls RHNA enforcement — Huntington Beach's fines only happen because the current enforcement posture is aggressive. A governor hostile to housing reform could gut the enforcement framework. The D fragmentation risk remains real: Hilton voters are 73% locked in vs. Becerra at 52%. Strategic voting matters — the most credibly pro-housing candidate who can make the top two is the priority.
